content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Teresa L. Stricker 
Interim City Attorney 
City of Santa Rosa 
I 00 Santa Rosa Avenue, Room 8 
Santa Rosa, CA 95404 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-16-161 
Dear Ms. Stricker: 
August 17, 2016 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the coÏflict of interest provisions of 
the Political Refonn Act (the "Act").1 This letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political 
Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when rendering advice. 
(bz re Oglesby ( 1975) I FPPC Ops. 7 I.) Please note that we arc only providing advice under the 
conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions 
such as common law conflict of interest or Section I 090. 
QUESTION 
Docs the Act prohibit the Mayor from taking part in governmental decisions relating to a 
proposed rent control ordinance that will impose "just cause" eviction controls on certain residential 
real properties given that the Mayor's spouse owns a single-family residence with an attached in­
law unit and that the Mayor separately rents out each of those residences? 
CONCLUSION 
Yes. The Act prohibits the Mayor from taking part in those decisions because they will have 
a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on his financial interest in his residential rental 
business distinguishable from their effect on the public generally. 
FACTS 
You are the Interim City Attorney for the City of Santa Rosa and the authorized 
representative of Mayor John Sawyer. The City Council will soon consider decisions relating to a 
1 The Political Refonn Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory rMferences are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regul11tions, unless otherwise indicated. 


File No. A-16-161 
Page No. 2 
proposed rent control ordinance imposing "just cause" eviction controls on certain residential real 
properties that will limit the permitted grounds for a landlord to evict a tenant. 
As currently drafted, the proposed rent control ordinance exempts certain residential real 
properties from its rent control and eviction control provisions, including, among others, single­
family homes with in-law units. There are approximately 68,803 residential real properties within 
the City, and the proposed rent control ordinance would apply to 11,076 of those properties. 
The Mayor's spouse owns a single-family residence with an attached in-law unit, and the 
Mayor separately rents out each of these residences to different tenants. Under the current draft of 
the proposed rent control ordinance the Mayor's spouse's single-family residence and attached in 
law-unit would be exempt from its eviction-control re!:,rulations. 
The City Council recently considered a temporary eviction control measure. In its 
deliberations over the temporary eviction control measure, it became clear that the Council may 
wish to amend the proposed rent control ordinance to extend its eviction-control regulations to 
single family homes, including in-law units, and to other types of residential real properties exempt 
from those regulations under the current draft of the proposed rent control ordinance. 
ANALYSIS 
The Act's conflict of interest provisions ensure that public officials will perfonn their duties 
in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the financial 
interests of persons who have supported them. (Section 8 100 I (b ). ) 
Section 87100 prohibits a public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise 
using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a 
financial interest. Section 87103 provides that a public official has a "financial interest" in a 
decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a 
material financial effect on one or more of the public official's interests that is distinguishable from 
the decision's effect on the public generally. 
Section 87103 also describes the interests from which a conflict of interest may arise under 
the Act,2 and of those interests, the facts presented and the decisions at issue implicate the 
following: 
• Any interest in a business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect investment of 
$2,000 or more (Section 87103(a)); or in which the official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, 
employee, or holds any position of management (Section 87103( d)). The Mayor has an interest 
in his business renting out his single-family residence and the attached in-law unit. 
? Any financial interest identified in Section 87103 of an immediate family member of the official may also 
give rise to a conflict of interest for that official under the Act. Therefore, the Mayor is financially interested in the 
single-family residence and attached inlaw unit owned by his spouse that are the basis of his financial interest in his 
residential rental business at issue here. 


File No. A-16-161 
Page No. 3 
• 
Any interest in real property in which the official has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 
or more. (Section 87103(b).) The Mayor has a real-property interest in his single-family 
residence and the attached in-law unit. 
• 
Any interest in a source of income to the official, including promised income, which 
aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c).) The 
Mayor has source-of-income interests in his residential rental business and in the each of the 
tenants of the single-family residence and the attached in-law unit. 
• 
An interest in the official's personal finances, including those of the official's immediate 
family, also known as the "personal financial effects" Rule. (Section 87103; Regulation 
18702.5.) An official always has an interest in his or her own personal finances. 
Foreseeability and Materiality 
For a financial interest not "explicitly involved"3 in the decision at issue, the financial effect 
of the decision on an official's interest is reasonably foreseeable if it can be recognized as a realistic 
possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical. (Regulation 18701 (b).) 
Regulation 18702.l(b) sets forth the materiality standard applicable to a decision's financial 
effect on an official's interest in a business entity that is not explicitly involved in the decision, and 
provides that such an effect is material if a prudent person with sufficient information would find it 
reasonably foreseeable that the effect would contribute to a change in the value of the business 
entity. Regulation 18702. l (b) also provides examples of decisions that may have a material effect 
on a business entity, including the example of a decision that would "[a]uthorize, prohibit, regulate 
or otherwise establish conditions for an activity in which the business entity is engaged." 
(Regulation 18702. l (b)(l ).) 
By imposing "just cause" eviction controls on certain residential properties within the 
jurisdiction, potentially including the Mayor's own single-family residence and attached in-law 
unit, the proposed ordinance would regulate or otherwise establish conditions for the rental of 
residential residences within the City, an activity in which the Mayor's residential rental business is 
engaged. Therefore, the decisions at issue will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial 
effect on the Mayor's financial interest in his residential rental business:' 
l An official's financial interest is explicitly involved in a governmental decision if the interest is a named 
party in, or subject of, the decision. A financial interest is the subject ofa proceeding if the decision involves the 
issuance, renewal, approval, denial. or revocation of any license, pennit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the 
financial interest, or if the decision affects a financial interest in a business entity described in Regulation 18702. l (a). 
(Regulations l8701(a) and 18702.l(a).) 
4 In light of our conclusion that the Mayor is disqualified based upon the reasonably and foreseeable material 
effect on his interest in his rental business, it is unnecessary to address the Mayor's other interests at this time. We note 
that, because his interest in real property is a rental property. the materiality standards for a business interest provided 
under Regulation 18702.1 also apply to the property. (Regulauons I 8702.2(a)(8).) 


File No. A-16-161 
Page No. 4 
"Public Generally" Exception 
Regulation I 8703(a) sets forth the "public generally" exception, and provides in pertinent 
part as follows: 
''A governmental decision's financial effect on a public official's financial 
interest is indistinguishable from its effect on the public generally if the 
official establishes that a significant se!,rment of the public is affected and the 
effect on his or her financial interest is not unique compared to the effect on 
the significant segment." 
Regulation I 8703(b )(2) provides that a significant segment of the public is at least 25 percent of all 
real property, commercial real property, or residential real property within the official's jurisdiction. 
Accordingly, the public generally exception requires that we determine whether the 
ordinance will effect at least 25 percent of all residential real properties in a manner that is not 
unique from the effect on the official. In this case, there are approximately 68,803 residential 
dwelling units within the City. As drafted, the proposed ordinance would impose "just cause" 
eviction controls on just I 1,076 of those units, or 16.1 percent. Based upon the facts provided, the 
Mayor may not invoke the "public generally" exception with respect to the proposed ordinance.5 
Thus, we conclude that the Act prohibits the Mayor from taking part in decisions relating to 
the proposed ordinance because they will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on 
his financial interest in his residential rental business. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
MFC:jgl 
Sincerely, 
By: 
Matthew F. Christy 
Counsel, Legal Division 
s The facts presented indicate that the City may consider amending the proposed rent control ordinance so that 
its eviction control regulations would additionally apply to residential real propenies that are currently exempt from 
their application under the current draft. When governmental decisions are incapable of segmentation. and one option 
(or more) under consideration fails to qualify for the "public generally" exception, no competing option under 
consideration may invoke the exception. (Mauus Advice Letter. No. A-16-113.)

business distinguishable from their effect on the public generally.

law unit and that the Mayor separately rents out each of those residences?
